{{Short description|Flow of electric charge through a conductor}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Electric current
| image = Ohm's Law with Voltage source.svg
| caption = A simple electrical circuit with a [[voltage source]] driving an electric current through a [[resistor]].
| topics = [[Electric charge]], [[Electric field]], [[Ohm's law]], [[Magnetic field]]
| symbol = I
| SI_unit = [[ampere]] (A)
| dimension = I
}}

An '''electric current''' is a flow of [[electric charge]] through a [[conductor (electrical)|conductor]]. In [[electrical circuit|electric circuits]], this charge is often carried by moving [[electron]]s in a wire. The [[SI unit]] for electric current is the [[ampere]] (A), which is defined as one [[coulomb]] of charge passing through a point per [[second]]:

:<math>I = \frac{dQ}{dt}</math>

== Ohm's law ==
{{Main|Ohm's law}}
In many conductive materials, the current <math>I</math> is proportional to the applied [[voltage]] <math>V</math>:
:<math>I = \frac{V}{R}</math>
where <math>R</math> is the [[electrical resistance]].

== Current density ==
The [[current density]] <math>\mathbf{J}</math> is a [[vector field]] describing current flow per unit area:
:<math>\mathbf{J} = nq\mathbf{v}_d</math>
where <math>n</math> is the charge carrier density, <math>q</math> is the charge of each carrier, and <math>\mathbf{v}_d</math> is the [[drift velocity]].

The total current through a surface is:
:<math>I = \int \mathbf{J} \cdot d\mathbf{A}</math>

== Types of current ==

=== Direct current (DC) ===
All [[electric charge]] flows in one constant direction. Common sources: [[Battery (electricity)|batteries]], [[solar cell]]s, [[DC power supply|DC power supplies]].

=== Alternating current (AC) ===
The direction of charge flow periodically reverses. The standard for mains electricity worldwide. The voltage varies sinusoidally:
:<math>V(t) = V_0\sin(2\pi ft)</math>
where <math>f</math> is the [[frequency]] (typically 50 or 60 Hz).

== Magnetic effects ==
An electric current produces a [[magnetic field]], as described by [[Ampère's circuital law]]:
:<math>\oint \mathbf{B} \cdot d\boldsymbol{\ell} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enc}}</math>

This is the principle behind [[electromagnet]]s, [[electric motor]]s, and [[transformer]]s.

== Conduction mechanisms ==
* '''Metallic conduction''' — [[electron]]s in the [[conduction band]]
* '''Electrolytic conduction''' — [[ion]]s in solution
* '''Semiconductor conduction''' — electrons and [[electron hole|holes]]
* '''Superconductivity''' — zero-resistance current below a critical temperature

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Ampere]]
* [[Electric charge]]
* [[Electric field]]
* [[Ohm's law]]
* [[Voltage]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Electric current| ]]
[[Category:Electromagnetism]]
[[Category:Physical quantities]]
